heroText: Riga is a key city in Latvia, combining landmarks, local culture, transport access and seasonal reasons to plan a visit.

highlights.name: Riga
highlights.text: Riga is a key city in Latvia, combining landmarks, local culture, transport access and seasonal reasons to plan a visit.

history.label: 12th century
history.text: The Livonian Chronicle of Henry testifies to Riga having long been a trading centre by the 12th century, referring to it as portus antiquus (ancient port), and describes dwellings and warehouses used to store.

Albert's difficulties with Riga's citizenry continued; with papal intervention, a settlement was reached in 1225 whereby they no longer had to pay tax to the Bishop of Riga, and Riga's citizens acquired the right.

He failed in his aspiration to be anointed archbishop but the German hegemony he established over the Livonia would last for seven centuries. Hanseatic League In 1282, Riga became a member of the Hanseatic.
In 1524, iconoclasts during iconoclast riots targeted a statue of the Virgin Mary in the cathedral to make a statement against religious icons.

In 1621, during the Polish–Swedish War (1621–1625), Riga and the outlying fortress of Daugavgrīva came under the rule of Gustavus Adolphus, King of Sweden, who intervened in the Thirty Years' War not only for.

history.label: 20th century
history.text: At the beginning of the 20th century, Riga was the largest timber export port in the Russian Empire and ranked the 3rd according to the external trade volume.

The nationalist movement of the Neo-Latvians was followed by the socialist New Current during the city's rapid industrialisation, culminating in the 1905 Revolution led by the Latvian Social Democratic Workers' Party. World War I.
Latvia, with Riga as its capital city, thus declared its independence on 18 November 1918.
The majority of the Baltic Germans were resettled in late 1939, prior to the occupation of Estonia and Latvia by the Soviet Union in June 1940. World War II and Soviet era During World.
The most notorious deportation, the June deportation, took place on 13 and 14 June 1941, estimated at 15,600 men, women, and children, and including 20% of Latvia's last legal government.
Peter's Church which lost its wooden tower after a fire caused by the Wehrmacht (renovated in 1954).

history.label: Regional role
history.text: The Danes landed in Livonia, built a fortress at Reval (Tallinn) and set about conquering Estonian and Livonian lands.
